content: Document ID: BASEL3-CR-IRB Title: Basel III Credit Risk Framework and IRB Concepts Section 1.1 - Credit Risk Objective Basel III describes credit risk as the possibility that a borrower or counterparty fails to meet obligations in accordance with agreed terms. The framework aims to ensure that regulated institutions maintain enough capital to absorb unexpected losses arising from that risk. Although Basel capital rules are written for prudential supervision rather than retail loan origination directly, their core concepts are highly relevant for internal risk measurement. These concepts include probability of default, loss given default, exposure at default, expected loss, and the distinction between regulatory capital and day-to-day credit decisioning. Section 2.1 - Probability of Default Probability of default, or PD, is the estimated likelihood that an obligor will default over a specified horizon, usually one year. In practice, PD models draw on repayment history, delinquency patterns, leverage, income stability, external credit bureau information, and other predictive factors. Good PD estimation requires sufficient historical depth, conservative calibration, and periodic back-testing. A lender should understand that PD is not a certainty statement. It is a risk estimate that supports pricing, approval thresholds, portfolio monitoring, and capital planning. Section 2.2 - Loss Given Default Loss given default, or LGD, is the proportion of exposure that would be lost if default occurs. LGD depends on recovery prospects, collateral type, seniority, enforcement efficiency, legal expenses, cure rates, and macroeconomic conditions. Unsecured retail lending generally exhibits higher LGD than secured lending because recoveries are more limited and more variable. Even when default probability is the main driver of underwriting, institutions should remain aware that two borrowers with similar PDs may produce different expected losses if the recovery environment differs materially. Section 2.3 - Exposure at Default Exposure at default, or EAD, measures the amount expected to be outstanding when default happens. For term loans, EAD is influenced by scheduled amortization and delinquent interest. For revolving credit products, undrawn commitments and borrower behavior near stress can increase EAD materially. Sound risk management therefore looks beyond current outstanding balance and considers likely utilization at the point of failure. Section 3.1 - Expected Loss and Unexpected Loss Expected loss is broadly the product of PD, LGD, and EAD. It reflects the average loss the institution anticipates over time and is often addressed through pricing, provisioning, or business planning. Unexpected loss represents adverse deviation around that expectation and is one reason capital buffers are necessary. A retail lender using credit scoring for underwriting does not need to replicate Basel capital formulas exactly, but it benefits from understanding the relationship between borrower default likelihood, recovery potential, and total portfolio volatility.

content: Unexpected loss represents adverse deviation around that expectation and is one reason capital buffers are necessary. A retail lender using credit scoring for underwriting does not need to replicate Basel capital formulas exactly, but it benefits from understanding the relationship between borrower default likelihood, recovery potential, and total portfolio volatility. Section 4.1 - Standardized and IRB Approaches Basel III allows different approaches to calculate regulatory capital. Under the standardized approach, risk weights are assigned using supervisory rules and external criteria. Under the internal ratings-based, or IRB, approach, banks use validated internal estimates of PD and, in more advanced settings, LGD and EAD as well. IRB requires strong governance, data lineage, model validation, and supervisory approval. The practical lesson for model builders is that internally generated scores must be disciplined, explainable, and monitored if they are to support material credit decisions. Section 4.2 - Model Governance Expectations An institution relying on internal risk grades or probability models should define the population, performance horizon, segmentation, overrides policy, and validation standards. Governance should cover training data quality, handling of missing values, feature stability, discrimination power, calibration drift, and periodic review of adverse outcomes. Overrides to model recommendations should be documented so management can understand whether human review is improving outcomes or weakening discipline. Section 5.1 - Use Test and Decision Integration Basel practice emphasizes the use test: internal ratings should not exist only for regulatory reporting; they should influence real business processes. That means a PD estimate or risk classification should inform underwriting, pricing, limits, collections strategy, and portfolio monitoring. When a borrower falls into a borderline zone, policy should define whether the institution declines, prices for risk, or escalates for manual review. The use test also implies that explanations need to be understandable to frontline credit officers. Section 6.1 - Conservatism and Cyclicality Credit risk is cyclical. Models trained on benign periods can understate stress behavior, while models trained only on downturn data can become excessively restrictive. Basel guidance therefore encourages conservative estimation, especially where data are thin or borrower conduct changes quickly. Institutions should be careful when relying on recent repayment stability alone if macro conditions, employment conditions, or funding costs are deteriorating. Section 7.1 - Retail Portfolio Application For retail lending, Basel concepts translate into practical controls: identify strong predictors of default, monitor delinquency migration, adjust for exposure growth, and review whether the portfolio mix has shifted toward thinner-file or higher-risk segments. A credit score can be valuable when it is paired with clear policy rules, exceptions handling, and documentation of why a given risk class leads to approval, rejection, or manual review.

content: Document ID: RBI-FPC-NBFC Title: RBI Fair Practices Code for NBFC Lending Operations Section 1.1 - Purpose and Scope The Fair Practices Code for non-banking financial companies establishes the minimum conduct standards that lenders must follow while originating, appraising, servicing, and recovering loans. The purpose of the code is not merely procedural. It is designed to make sure that the borrower understands the product, the charges, the key risks, and the reasons behind the lender's decision. A compliant lender is expected to provide information in a way that is timely, understandable, and consistent across the customer journey. This includes marketing communication, application forms, sanction letters, account statements, repayment reminders, and recovery notices. Section 2.1 - Loan Application Standards Loan application forms must capture the information necessary for a proper credit decision and must clearly disclose the documents needed for assessment. The lender should explain what information is mandatory, what information is optional, and how the information will be used. A borrower should not be asked to sign incomplete forms or blank schedules. If credit bureau reports, bank statements, income declarations, employer records, or asset details are required, the lender should state this upfront. The application stage should also disclose processing charges, the likely turnaround time, and the manner in which decisions or delays will be communicated. Section 3.1 - Credit Appraisal Principles Credit appraisal must be based on a documented evaluation of the borrower's repayment capacity, stability of income, existing indebtedness, and relevant credit history. Appraisal should be proportionate to the loan product and risk profile. For smaller retail loans, this may rely on salary credits, self-employment cash flows, repayment behavior, and credit bureau records. For larger exposures, the assessment may also review leverage, collateral support, guarantor strength, or business seasonality. The key principle is that the lender should demonstrate that the final decision is supported by facts rather than arbitrary preference. Section 3.2 - Responsible Use of Credit Information The code expects lenders to use credit bureau information responsibly. Bureau data should be treated as an input into appraisal, not as the sole determinant. Negative bureau history should be reviewed in context, including account age, delay severity, cure behavior, and the recency of delinquency. Positive repayment history, stable account conduct, and declining leverage should also be considered. A lender that uses automated scoring should ensure that the score can be interpreted by internal decision makers and that exceptions are escalated for manual review where appropriate. Section 4.1 - Sanction Communication When a loan is approved, the sanction letter should state the amount, tenor, annualized interest rate, method of application of interest, installment amount if applicable, applicable fees, penal charges policy, and material conditions precedent to disbursement. Borrowers should be given the opportunity to review the terms before acceptance. Material changes after sanction should not be introduced without clear notice and lawful basis. If the loan uses floating rates or variable charges, the mechanism for revision must be transparent.

content: charges policy, and material conditions precedent to disbursement. Borrowers should be given the opportunity to review the terms before acceptance. Material changes after sanction should not be introduced without clear notice and lawful basis. If the loan uses floating rates or variable charges, the mechanism for revision must be transparent. Section 4.2 - Rejection Communication The Fair Practices Code emphasizes that rejected applicants should be informed in an appropriate manner. While detailed internal scoring models need not be disclosed, the lender should communicate the broad basis of rejection where policy permits. Common reasons may include inadequate repayment capacity, adverse credit history, insufficient documentation, or risk policy exceptions. Communication should be respectful, free of stigmatizing language, and recorded in the lender's system. The objective is to prevent opaque or arbitrary rejection practices. Section 5.1 - Interest Rate and Charge Transparency Interest rates and all material charges must be disclosed in a form the borrower can understand. The lender should explain whether the rate is fixed or floating, whether penal charges are applied on overdue amounts, and whether charges vary by borrower category. The board-approved interest rate model should be available internally and should support fair treatment across similarly situated customers. Excessively complex or hidden fee structures are inconsistent with the code's intent. Section 6.1 - Post-Disbursement Conduct Once the loan is disbursed, the lender must continue to treat the borrower fairly. Statements, reminders, and notices should be accurate and timely. Changes in terms must follow the sanction conditions and applicable law. Account servicing should allow the borrower to understand outstanding principal, accrued interest, overdue amounts, and the consequences of non-payment. If collection action becomes necessary, the lender is expected to act within law and with civility. Section 6.2 - Recovery and Customer Dignity Recovery agents and collection staff must avoid harassment, intimidation, or intrusive conduct. Contacts should occur at reasonable times, and conversations should remain limited to recovery-related matters. A lender should maintain oversight on outsourced recovery activity and should record complaints or allegations of misconduct. Distressed borrowers may require restructuring discussions or supervisory review before aggressive recovery measures are considered. Section 7.1 - Governance and Audit Trail A financial institution should maintain a clear audit trail for how the decision was reached, what documents were reviewed, which risk indicators were considered, and how communication was delivered. For AI-assisted or score-driven decisions, the code's expectations imply that institutions should preserve enough explanation to support internal review, customer grievance handling, and supervisory inspection. Human oversight remains important where the case is borderline, inconsistent, or unusually sensitive.

content: Document ID: RBI-RESPONSIBLE-LENDING Title: RBI Themes on Responsible Lending, KYC, and Credit Information Use Section 1.1 - Responsible Lending Foundation Responsible lending requires that a financial institution assess affordability, disclose material terms, verify borrower identity, and avoid extending credit in a way that predictably causes distress. The idea is broader than compliance with documentation alone. A lender should understand the source of repayment, the purpose of the credit, the borrower's overall debt burden, and whether the proposed product matches the customer's profile. This is especially important for digitally originated loans and high-volume retail programs where automation can otherwise turn underwriting into a mechanical exercise. Section 2.1 - Know Your Customer Requirements KYC controls require the lender to establish and verify the customer's identity using approved documents, validate address and contact details where required, and understand the nature of the customer relationship. Effective KYC is not merely an onboarding checklist. It supports fraud control, duplicate detection, sanctions compliance, and traceability of the credit relationship. A weak KYC process can undermine credit quality because false identities or misrepresented profiles distort both the underwriting model and subsequent recovery efforts. Section 2.2 - Customer Due Diligence in Credit Decisions Customer due diligence should be proportionate to risk. For salaried borrowers, this may include employer verification, salary credit patterns, and consistency between declared occupation and account behavior. For self-employed applicants, turnover, seasonality, tax records, or banking patterns may be reviewed. When the institution relies on third-party data sources, it remains responsible for ensuring that those data are appropriate and current for the decision being made. Section 3.1 - Credit Bureau Usage Credit bureau reports are central to responsible lending because they help the lender understand current indebtedness, prior repayment conduct, and the potential for overextension. However, bureau usage must remain disciplined. Data should be obtained lawfully, matched accurately, and interpreted contextually. A short isolated delay is different from repeated severe delinquency, and a long stable credit history may offset some concerns that a thin-file applicant cannot address. Bureau inquiries, active tradelines, utilization, and write-off history all provide signals, but the institution should avoid simplistic one-variable decisions. Section 3.2 - Fair Treatment and Explainability When credit is denied or escalated, the institution should be able to articulate the broad reasons in a fair and respectful manner. Responsible lending practice supports the idea that the customer should not be left with a completely opaque outcome. The institution need not disclose proprietary scoring logic in detail, but it should know internally whether the main drivers were leverage, delinquency history, insufficient income coverage, policy breach, or documentation gaps. Explainability also supports internal review, complaint handling, and conduct oversight.

content: should not be left with a completely opaque outcome. The institution need not disclose proprietary scoring logic in detail, but it should know internally whether the main drivers were leverage, delinquency history, insufficient income coverage, policy breach, or documentation gaps. Explainability also supports internal review, complaint handling, and conduct oversight. Section 4.1 - Affordability Assessment Affordability requires more than checking whether the applicant can technically fit a monthly installment into current income. It should consider debt obligations already outstanding, variability of earnings, family size, living expenses where relevant, and the effect of interest rate or income shocks. A borrower near the limit of affordability may warrant either a smaller sanctioned amount, additional review, or decline. This principle aligns naturally with risk scores that flag borderline cases rather than forcing automatic approval. Section 4.2 - Product Suitability and Risk Controls Responsible lenders should match product structure to borrower profile. Short-tenor high-cost products may be unsuitable for borrowers with unstable income. Revolving credit may create greater risk if the borrower already shows utilization stress or prior payment irregularity. Suitability considerations can be embedded in policy rules or decision-support reports so that a strong score in one dimension does not override clear concerns in another. Section 5.1 - Data Governance and Consent Institutions should maintain lawful data collection, proper customer consent where applicable, secure storage, and limited access to sensitive information. Automated scoring systems should preserve data lineage so that the lender knows which source contributed which field. If a bureau field, bank statement attribute, or derived affordability ratio materially affected the decision, that should be auditable for internal governance. Section 6.1 - Manual Review and Escalation Responsible lending frameworks typically recognize that some cases are not well served by purely automated decisions. Borderline affordability, conflicting data, thin files with high requested exposure, or inconsistent bureau patterns may require manual review by a credit officer. Escalation is not a model failure. It is a governance control that prevents overconfidence in uncertain cases and allows human judgment to consider contextual evidence. Section 7.1 - Practical Decision Support Implications For an AI-assisted lending system, responsible practice means combining predictive scoring with policy checks, regulatory context, and transparent reporting. The system should identify the main risk drivers, surface relevant regulatory guidance, and clearly distinguish between low-risk approvals, high-risk rejections, and uncertain cases that should be manually reviewed. This preserves both efficiency and control while keeping the final lending responsibility with the institution rather than the model alone.

content: Document ID: NBFC-PRUDENTIAL-NORMS Title: RBI and Prudential Norm Themes Relevant to NBFC Credit Risk Management Section 1.1 - Prudential Oversight Context NBFC prudential regulation focuses on the safety, soundness, and resilience of institutions that lend outside the traditional banking structure. Supervisory attention typically covers capital adequacy, asset classification, provisioning, concentration, governance, customer treatment, and reporting discipline. While the detailed circular landscape changes over time, a lender evaluating retail credit should understand that prudential norms are not only accounting rules. They shape how early stress is recognized, escalations are handled, and losses are provisioned. Section 2.1 - Asset Classification Logic Asset classification determines whether a loan is treated as standard, special mention, or non-performing based on the borrower's payment behavior and contractual delinquency. The core prudential idea is that credit weakness should be recognized on time and should not be masked by repeated restructuring without evidence of genuine cure. Institutions should have clear rules for identifying days past due, partial payments, and technical versus substantive arrears. Data quality is critical because provisioning and supervisory reporting depend on the accuracy of these status fields. Section 2.2 - NPA Recognition For many lending products, an account becomes a non-performing asset when interest or principal remains overdue beyond the applicable prudential threshold. Once an account crosses into NPA status, the lender should intensify monitoring, reassess recoverability, and evaluate whether income recognition or accrual practices must change. The transition is important for retail risk systems because borrowers with persistent delinquency or recent cure from deep arrears may warrant stricter underwriting treatment even if they are applying for fresh credit elsewhere. Section 3.1 - Provisioning Norms Provisioning norms require lenders to set aside reserves against expected deterioration in asset quality. Standard assets may attract lower provisioning, while substandard, doubtful, and loss assets require progressively higher coverage depending on the degree of impairment and the quality of security available. From a credit decision support perspective, the lesson is that delinquency severity, duration, and collateral quality all matter. A score focused only on origination probability may miss the economic cost of future deterioration if recovery prospects are ignored. Section 3.2 - Restructuring and Forbearance Prudential expectations generally require careful control over restructuring. A lender should not use repeated tenor extensions or payment holidays to delay recognition of underlying stress. When an account is modified, the institution should document the rationale, assess the borrower's revised repayment capacity, and monitor post-restructuring performance. For underwriting models, prior restructuring can be a meaningful risk signal because it suggests that the borrower has already encountered affordability challenges. Section 4.1 - Governance of Credit Sanctioning An NBFC should maintain approval matrices, delegated authorities, portfolio limits, and exception tracking. Credit decisions should be consistent with board-approved risk appetite and internal policy. If models are used, management should understand their limitations, approved use cases, and override rules. High-risk approvals outside policy tolerance should require escalation. Repeat exceptions in the same segment may indicate that the scorecard or policy thresholds need recalibration.

Section 3.1 - Provisioning Norms Provisioning norms require lenders to set aside reserves against expected deterioration in asset quality. Standard assets may attract lower provisioning, while substandard, doubtful, and loss assets require progressively higher coverage depending on the degree of impairment and the quality of security available. From a credit decision support perspective, the lesson is that delinquency severity, duration, and collateral quality all matter. A score focused only on origination probability may miss the economic cost of future deterioration if recovery prospects are ignored. Section 3.2 - Restructuring and Forbearance Prudential expectations generally require careful control over restructuring. A lender should not use repeated tenor extensions or payment holidays to delay recognition of underlying stress. When an account is modified, the institution should document the rationale, assess the borrower's revised repayment capacity, and monitor post-restructuring performance. For underwriting models, prior restructuring can be a meaningful risk signal because it suggests that the borrower has already encountered affordability challenges. Section 4.1 - Governance of Credit Sanctioning An NBFC should maintain approval matrices, delegated authorities, portfolio limits, and exception tracking. Credit decisions should be consistent with board-approved risk appetite and internal policy. If models are used, management should understand their limitations, approved use cases, and override rules. High-risk approvals outside policy tolerance should require escalation. Repeat exceptions in the same segment may indicate that the scorecard or policy thresholds need recalibration.

content: decisions should be consistent with board-approved risk appetite and internal policy. If models are used, management should understand their limitations, approved use cases, and override rules. High-risk approvals outside policy tolerance should require escalation. Repeat exceptions in the same segment may indicate that the scorecard or policy thresholds need recalibration. Section 4.2 - Delinquency Monitoring and Collections Institutions are expected to monitor early warning signs such as roll-rate deterioration, missed installment clusters, rising utilization, or repeated bounce behavior. Collections strategy should be risk-sensitive and lawful. Borrowers who are only marginally delinquent may be suitable for reminders or restructuring assessment, while deeper delinquency requires stronger recovery action and provisioning review. Systems should preserve the dates and amounts relevant to delinquency measurement because these fields feed both operational control and regulatory reporting. Section 5.1 - Concentration and Portfolio View Prudential management is not only about individual borrowers. It also considers concentrations by geography, occupation, borrower type, product type, and risk band. A model that performs adequately on average may still expose the institution to concentrated downside if approvals cluster in unstable segments. Decision support systems should therefore retain segment labels and support downstream portfolio analytics, not just one-off approval outcomes. Section 6.1 - Documentation and Supervisory Traceability Prudential compliance depends on traceability. A lender should be able to show what information was used, how the risk decision was derived, what policy applied, and what follow-up action occurred once stress appeared. This matters for both traditional credit committees and AI-assisted scoring systems. Where automated recommendations are used, internal control teams should still be able to inspect inputs, model outputs, overrides, and resulting customer action. Section 7.1 - Practical Implication for Lending Decisions In practical underwriting, prudential norms support a simple discipline: identify the borrower's capacity and willingness to repay, understand prior signs of stress, classify risk accurately, and escalate borderline cases before loss emerges. A system that flags manual review for uncertain applicants is often more consistent with prudential practice than a system that forces binary approve or reject decisions in every case.
